context.description: The author argues that consciousness may perceive three-dimensional space in a way that exceeds normal visual input, drawing on reports of out-of-body and vision-like episodes. A section titled "Wir irren umher" introduces the idea that such experiences have long been explored in occult groups and religions.
normalization_notes: Translated section heading to English; kept source title transliterated as provided by the file name context.

rows.paragraph_text: Visual manipulation can create images that show an object in full or stitch together 360-degree panoramas, but such attempts still produce heavily distorted representations that make perception difficult.

The text describes reports, confirmed by the author’s own experience, in which consciousness suddenly perceives a three-dimensional space in its entirety, sometimes together with out-of-body experiences or brief vision-like episodes, followed by an inability to remember the moment accurately.
The author rejects a purely deceptive explanation and argues that the recurring reports point to a real perceptual capacity that is not normally accessible to conscious control.
The section opening notes that this line of inquiry has long been pursued by others, including closed occult groups and religions concerned with mastery of certain powers.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy philosophy_of_consciousness Book page on consciousness, perception, and out-of-body experiences. Section heading: Wir irren umher. The author discusses limits of visual representation, claims of whole-space perception, and the historical pursuit of these ideas in occult and religious settings.
